title: OpenAI Assistants API
learningObjectives: Understand the Assistants API object model: Assistants, Threads, Messages, Runs
Create and configure an Assistant with tools
Use File Search for document Q&A
Use Code Interpreter for data analysis
Stream Run outputs and handle tool calls

sections.title: Why Use the Assistants API
sections.content: Building an agent from scratch (managing message history, tool dispatch, state) is powerful but complex. The Assistants API is OpenAI's managed agent infrastructure: it handles thread/message persistence, runs the tool loop automatically, provides Code Interpreter (sandboxed Python execution) and File Search (managed RAG over uploaded documents) as built-in tools, and lets you focus on the assistant's behavior rather than infrastructure. Trade-off: you give up some control and flexibility for reduced boilerplate. The Assistants API is best when you need a quick, production-ready assistant with file understanding and code execution.

sections.title: Assistants API Object Model
sections.content:
**Four core objects:**

```
Assistant  — persistent entity with instructions + tools + model
    │
    │  (a user's conversation)
    ▼
Thread     — persistent conversation (sequence of messages)
    │
    │  (add messages + execute)
    ▼
Message    — user or assistant message with content + attachments
    │
    │  (async execution of assistant on thread)
    ▼
Run        — one execution of the assistant on a thread
            Statuses: queued → in_progress → requires_action
                      → completed | failed | cancelled | expired
```

**Built-in tools:**

| Tool | What it does |
|------|-------------|
| `file_search` | Semantic search over uploaded files (managed RAG) |
| `code_interpreter` | Sandboxed Python execution + file generation |
| Custom function tools | Your own function definitions (same as Day 28) |

**File Search internals:**
- Upload files → create a Vector Store → attach to Assistant or Thread
- Supported: PDF, DOCX, TXT, Markdown, HTML, JSON, and more
- Automatically chunks, embeds, and indexes
- Token cost: $0.10/GB/day for stored vectors

**Code Interpreter:**
- Runs Python in an OpenAI-managed sandbox
- Can create files (charts, processed data) and attach them to messages
- Has access to common data science libraries (pandas, numpy, matplotlib)
- Costs extra per session

**Run lifecycle:**
```python
# Create and poll a run
run = client.beta.threads.runs.create(thread_id=thread.id, assistant_id=assistant.id)
while run.status in ('queued', 'in_progress'):
    time.sleep(1)
    run = client.beta.threads.runs.retrieve(thread_id=thread.id, run_id=run.id)

# Or use streaming (preferred)
with client.beta.threads.runs.stream(
    thread_id=thread.id,
    assistant_id=assistant.id,
) as stream:
    for event in stream:
        ...
```

**When `requires_action` status:**
```python
if run.status == 'requires_action':
    tool_calls = run.required_action.submit_tool_outputs.tool_calls
    outputs = []
    for tc in tool_calls:
        result = dispatch_tool(tc.function.name, json.loads(tc.function.arguments))
        outputs.append({'tool_call_id': tc.id, 'output': result})
    run = client.beta.threads.runs.submit_tool_outputs(
        thread_id=thread.id, run_id=run.id, tool_outputs=outputs
    )
```

sections.title: Assistants API — Complete Implementation

items.mistake: Not polling or streaming — just checking status once
items.why: Runs are asynchronous. Checking status once immediately after creating a run will always find it in 'queued' or 'in_progress'. You must loop until a terminal status.
items.fix: Always poll in a loop (with sleep) or use the streaming API which handles this automatically.

items.mistake: Creating a new assistant for every user
items.why: Assistants are persistent, reusable configurations. Creating one per user is wasteful and makes updates hard (you'd need to update thousands of assistants).
items.fix: Create one assistant per use case (e.g., 'Customer Support Bot'). Create a new Thread per user session. Threads are cheap and isolated.

items.mistake: Forgetting to submit tool outputs on requires_action
items.why: If the run status is 'requires_action' and you don't submit tool outputs within the timeout, the run expires. The conversation is broken.
items.fix: Always handle 'requires_action' in your polling loop. Execute all tool calls, gather outputs, and call submit_tool_outputs before the run expires.

items.mistake: Not cleaning up unused threads and files
items.why: Threads are stored indefinitely by default, and files cost storage. A high-traffic app can accumulate thousands of stale threads and gigabytes of old files.
items.fix: Delete threads after sessions end. Set up a cleanup job to delete files older than N days. Monitor storage costs in the OpenAI dashboard.

sections.title: Interview Questions

conceptual.question: What are the four main objects in the Assistants API and how do they relate to each other?
conceptual.answer: Assistant: persistent configuration with instructions, model, and tools. Created once, reused across many conversations. Thread: a persistent conversation container — created per user session, holds all messages. One assistant can have many concurrent threads. Message: a single turn (user or assistant) within a thread, with text content and optional file attachments. Run: an asynchronous execution of the assistant on a thread. A single thread can have many sequential runs (one per user message). The lifecycle: create Assistant → create Thread per session → add Messages → create Run → poll until complete → read new Messages.
followUps: When would you use threads with multiple runs vs a single long run?

conceptual.question: What is the difference between the Assistants API's File Search and building your own RAG?
conceptual.answer: File Search: managed, zero-infrastructure — upload files, create a Vector Store, attach to assistant. OpenAI handles chunking, embedding, indexing, and retrieval. Limited control: can't tune chunk size, embedding model, retrieval strategy, or reranking. Custom RAG (Day 23-27): full control over every parameter — chunk size, overlap, embedding model, retrieval strategy (MMR, reranking, hybrid search), metadata filtering. Required when you need: specific chunking for structured documents, metadata-based access control, custom embedding models, or retrieval strategies not supported by File Search. For most use cases, File Search is sufficient and much faster to ship.
followUps: How would you migrate from File Search to custom RAG as requirements grow?

codeBased.question: How do you handle the 'requires_action' run status?
codeBased.answer:
```python
if run.status == 'requires_action':
    tool_calls = run.required_action.submit_tool_outputs.tool_calls
    outputs = []
    for tc in tool_calls:
        args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments)
        fn = TOOL_REGISTRY.get(tc.function.name)
        result = fn(**args) if fn else json.dumps({'error': 'Unknown tool'})
        outputs.append({'tool_call_id': tc.id, 'output': json.dumps(result)})
    
    # Submit all outputs in one call
    run = client.beta.threads.runs.submit_tool_outputs(
        thread_id=thread_id,
        run_id=run.id,
        tool_outputs=outputs,
    )
    # Continue polling — run will resume
```
All tool outputs must be submitted in a single `submit_tool_outputs` call.

seniorScenario.question: You're building a customer support assistant using the Assistants API that needs to: answer product FAQ from a 500-page PDF, look up order status via API, and escalate to humans for complex issues. How do you architect this?
seniorScenario.answer: Architecture: (1) Assistant: one Assistant with instructions ('You are TechCorp support. Answer FAQ from documents. Look up order status when asked. If you cannot resolve in 3 attempts, escalate.'). Tools: file_search (for FAQ PDF), get_order_status function, create_escalation_ticket function. (2) Vector Store: upload the 500-page PDF once, create a Vector Store. Attach to the assistant. No per-user indexing needed. (3) Thread: one Thread per customer session. Tag with session_id, user_id metadata. (4) Tool functions: get_order_status(order_id) → calls your order management API, returns JSON. create_escalation_ticket(issue_summary, conversation_summary) → creates a Zendesk ticket, notifies a human agent, marks thread as escalated. (5) Session lifecycle: create Thread on first contact. Keep thread_id in your session store (Redis, keyed by session_id). On new message: add to thread, run assistant, return response. Delete thread after session ends (24h TTL). (6) Monitoring: log every run's cost (from usage field), response time, and whether escalation was triggered.
followUps: How do you handle concurrent sessions from the same user on multiple devices?

wrongAnswers: A Run processes one message at a time and cannot call multiple tools — Runs can handle parallel tool calls in a single requires_action event
File Search in the Assistants API works the same as retrieving any attached file — it performs vector-based semantic search over the file's content, not full-file retrieval
Threads persist indefinitely for free — Threads are stored persistently and charged for storage if you keep them; delete unused threads to avoid accumulation costs

questions.question: In the Assistants API, what should you create once and reuse vs create per user session?

options.A: Create Thread once, create Assistant per session
options.B: Create Assistant once, create Thread per session
options.C: Create both Assistant and Thread once globally
options.D: Create Run once, reuse for all sessions

questions.explanation: Assistants are persistent configurations (instructions + tools) — create once, reuse across all users. Threads are conversation containers — create a new one per user session for isolation. This is the correct pattern in the Assistants API.

questions.question: A Run is in 'requires_action' status. What must you do to allow the run to continue?

options.A: Wait — the run will automatically retry the tool call
options.B: Execute the requested tool calls and submit all outputs via submit_tool_outputs
options.C: Cancel the run and create a new one
options.D: Add a new user message to the thread

questions.explanation: requires_action means the assistant decided to call your function tools. You must execute the functions, collect their outputs, and submit all outputs in a single submit_tool_outputs call. The run then continues processing.

questions.question: What is the advantage of streaming Runs vs polling?

options.B: Streaming shows tokens as they generate, enabling faster perceived response in UI

questions.explanation: Streaming delivers token delta events as they're generated, so the UI can display text progressively rather than waiting for the complete response. This dramatically improves perceived latency — users see output after 500ms instead of 3+ seconds.

questions.question: Your Assistants API app's storage costs are unexpectedly high. What is the likely cause?

options.A: Too many API calls per run
options.B: Threads and Vector Store files accumulating without cleanup
options.D: Streaming uses more tokens than polling

questions.explanation: Threads persist indefinitely unless explicitly deleted, and Vector Store files are charged at $0.10/GB/day. A high-traffic app creates thousands of threads and many large Vector Stores that accumulate over time. Implement thread TTLs and regular cleanup jobs.

questions.question: What is the Assistants API's 'code_interpreter' tool?

options.A: A tool that executes code by calling your own code execution service
options.B: OpenAI-managed sandboxed Python execution environment with data science libraries
options.C: A code review and syntax checking tool
options.D: A static analysis tool that evaluates code quality

questions.explanation: Code Interpreter is a fully managed sandboxed Python environment hosted by OpenAI. It has pandas, numpy, matplotlib and other data science libraries. The assistant can write and run Python, process files, and generate charts — returned as file attachments in messages.
